_meta.label: Astroport
_meta.summary: Primary AMM DEX on Terra phoenix-1. Three pool types matter for our pricing: constant-product (XYK), passive concentrated liquidity (PCL), and stableswap. Pool mechanics determine whether a reserve-ratio price is valid.

facts.claim: Constant-product (XYK) pools keep reserves balanced by Rx·Ry=k. Liquidity is spread across all prices, so they trade at any price even in large moves. Best for volatile pairs.

For an XYK pool, the spot price of one token in terms of the other equals the reserve ratio (Ry/Rx). This is why our reserve-ratio anchor method is valid for XYK pools: price_token = (reserve_anchor / reserve_token) × anchor_price.
facts.notes: Validated empirically: anchor method landed within 0.1-2.5% of known prices on XYK anchor/anchor pools.

facts.claim: Stableswap pools use an amplified invariant (amplification parameter A) that sits between constant-product and constant-price curves, concentrating liquidity near a 1:1 exchange rate for low slippage on stable pairs.

For stableswap pools the reserve ratio is NOT the price ratio (the amplified curve deliberately holds price near 1:1 across unequal reserves). Reserve-ratio pricing must be skipped for stableswap pools or it returns wrong prices.
facts.notes: Confirmed empirically: USDC/USDt stableswap pool gave a 5.3% error under reserve-ratio pricing. Our token-catalog cron skips pools whose type contains 'stable'.

facts.claim: Passive Concentrated Liquidity (PCL) pools cluster liquidity around a 'Price Scale' and track an internal 'Price Oracle' — an exponential moving average of recent trade prices governed by an ema_half_time parameter.

A PCL pool re-pegs its Price Scale toward the Price Oracle only when accrued fees are enough to offset at least half the potential impermanent loss from re-pegging. Until it can, it offers slightly worse execution than a plain XYK pool.

Because a PCL pool's price tracks an EMA oracle and only re-pegs when fee-justified, its instantaneous reserve ratio can lag the true spot price during fast moves. A PCL pool-derived price is therefore a weaker spot read than an XYK one and may diverge from other sources.
facts.notes: Relevant when a pool-implied price disagrees with TLA/CoinGecko: a PCL pool can legitimately lag, so the disagreement may be the pool, not the other sources.

facts.claim: PCL pools raise transaction fees as volatility widens the gap between Price Scale and Price Oracle, distributing the extra fees to liquidity providers as impermanent-loss compensation.

PCL reduces impermanent loss for price moves within its concentrated range, but on a tail event (price moving far outside the range) IL and slippage can be severe because liquidity becomes scarce there. Re-pegging mitigates but never eliminates IL; LPs remain exposed to unrealized IL accrued before a re-peg.

XYK pools spread liquidity (and IL risk) evenly across the whole price range. They are more exposed to routine impermanent loss than in-range PCL, but retain liquidity and degrade more gracefully during extreme moves.

Astroport's tokens.byChain API returns a priceUsd per token that occasionally reflects a mispricing for derivative assets (e.g. it has priced bLUNA at ~LUNA's price rather than applying bLUNA's redemption ratio). We report Astroport's value as one source and let cross-source divergence flag it; we do not silently correct or hide it.
facts.notes: Observed in our run: Astroport bLUNA priceUsd ≈ LUNA price, ~44% below TLA's bLUNA price. Confirmed it is Astroport's own data via the denom key, not our parsing bug. Multi-source design surfaces this as wide_divergence.

facts.claim: The current Astroport priceUsd for a given token, as captured in our pricing snapshot.
facts.notes: Per-token live value from the token-catalog snapshot; never hardcoded.

facts.claim: Astroport is the primary DEX whose LP pools TLA incentivizes; LP and ampLP positions in TLA buckets are Astroport pools. Pool reserves drive both DEX prices and LP/ampLP intrinsic valuation.
